quote_of_day.text:
>>3430
I mean if you throw a genuinely smart soul in the Indian school system, and they're not STEM oriented or ready to play the game they'll honestly be raped by anything and everyone. Its brutal.
quote_of_day.name: Anon

articles.title: Weather on the board today
articles.body:
A low-pressure system of isolation and disconnection moved over the board, as Anon shared a personal account of feeling like a stranger in their own life. This drew a scattered response but remained a steady presence.

A brief but intense downpour of images occurred in the afternoon, as one Anon invited others to share recent photos from their rolls. This burst of activity was short-lived but garnered some attention.

Meanwhile, a cold front of criticism blew in from the west, as an Anon shared a link to a Reddit thread on 'anti-gay propaganda' and expressed disillusionment with the notion of 'safe spaces'.

Elsewhere, a drizzle of musings on gobar and caste occurred, with one Anon noting the potential benefits of controlled gobar.

A separate thread on India's history of invasions and infighting also ran, with one Anon listing various historical events in a tone of lament.

articles.title: Anon's isolated life
articles.body:
Anon described feeling like a stranger their entire life, shaped by their eclectic parents and an unusual upbringing on a wind-struck island with limited internet access.

Their experience in Indian high school was difficult to gauge from the post, but they mentioned struggling socially in college. Anon wasn't the only one who felt isolated by Indian institutions.

Another anon noted that Indian teachers, particularly older ones, can be authoritarian and enjoy exerting control over students. This poster described their own experience working with such teachers, characterizing them as qualifying for "psychopathy".

One anon disagreed that school systems play a significant role in shaping young people. Instead, they argued that self-interest in subjects that genuinely interest them drives kids' development.

This interest comes from exposure and a better understanding of the world, not validation or social pressure. However, others disputed this view.

Anon pointed out that India has an overabundance of students enrolling in engineering and medical courses, suggesting that there may be external factors at play. Anon's own experience in Indian institutions was harsh.

They felt like they missed out on a development period and no longer identify as Indian, despite loving the culture.

They noted that, outside of India, they find other Indians to be great and sweet, but inside, they find them to be awful. The discussion also touched on the challenges faced by students who don't fit into the Indian system.

One anon noted that smart students who aren't STEM-oriented or willing to "play the game" can be brutalized by the system.

The thread drew various responses, from those who could relate to Anon's experience to those who made more general points about Indian culture and institutions. Anon also got asked several personal questions.

articles.title: Anon shares photos from roll
articles.body: Anon kicked off the thread by asking others to post recent photos from their roll, and then offered to start. One anon responded with a photo from Peak Valley, taken a couple of months back, although they noted they don't usually take photos in 'pajeetland'. Another anon expressed eagerness to see the photo, jokingly warning that it might get moved to '/kala/', and asking where it was.

articles.title: Anon Shares Link to Reddit Thread on 'Anti-Gay Propaganda'
articles.body:
Anon posted a link to a Reddit thread allegedly containing 'anti-gay propaganda' and expressed disappointment that Reddit, often considered a 'safe space,' would host such content.

Another anon responded by criticizing 'pajeets' for discovering and normalizing Reddit, suggesting it was detrimental to the platform.

One respondent made a comment about 'ugly bastard cuck porn,' seemingly in reference to the content of the linked thread.

Anon also brought up Rakul, making a remark about her being a 'tranny chamroid,' though the context and relevance of this statement were unclear.

The conversation also touched on leftists and their apparent affinity for certain types of content, with one anon simply asking, 'Why leftists like dis?' The thread included various other comments, including one expressing frustration that the imageboard had been 'ruined' and another remarking on the presence of Dalits on Reddit.

One anon also made a cryptic statement, 'India is bioweapon.'

articles.title: Anon muses on gobar and caste
articles.body:
Anon posted 'Uncontrolled gobar creates a havoc, controlled one wins you the world!' Another anon responded with 'She looks so cute and beautiful why did she even steal, couldve just asked nicely' in reference to an image.

A respondent called someone a 'gobarpyari' and made an obscene remark about their mother. Anon also mentioned being a 'gobarbhakshak' whose dick twitched at the sight of an image of a woman.

One anon expressed puzzlement that 'japnese have fairly good view indians.'

articles.title: Anon bemoans India's history of invasions and infighting
articles.body:
Anon listed various invasions and events in Indian history, including the Aryan invasion, Muslim invasion, European invasion, and freedom struggle.

They claimed that those who pride themselves on India's past rarely discuss the humiliation endured to reach the current year.

Another anon responded, criticizing India's internal divisions, stating that Europeans and Arabs can unite when it matters, unlike Indians who are fractured along caste, religion, and ideological lines.

Anon exchanges followed, with one saying Indians are 'honest and vocal' about caste, and another claiming Europeans and Arabs have histories of enslaving and slaughtering each other.
